resources.string: Use this phone as its own wireless debugging host. No extra ADB terminal app is required.
Use this phone to connect to and control a different Android phone over wireless debugging.
Self mode keeps the ADB host inside this app. Turn on wireless debugging on this phone, note the pairing and connect ports, then come back here to pair and run the mute command.
On the target phone, open Developer options > Wireless debugging, then enter the IP address, pairing port, connect port, and the 6-digit pairing code shown there.

1. Connect this phone to Wi-Fi.
2. In Security and privacy, turn off Auto Blocker if Samsung is blocking ADB tools.
3. In Phone info > Software information, tap Build number 7 times.
4. Open Developer options and turn on Wireless debugging.
5. Tap Pair device with pairing code and copy the pairing port, connect port, and 6-digit code.
6. Return here, tap Pair + Connect, then use Mute Camera or Unmute Camera.

1. Connect both phones to the same Wi-Fi network.
2. On the target Samsung phone, turn off Auto Blocker if it is blocking ADB tools.
3. On the target phone, enable Developer options and Wireless debugging.
4. On the target phone, tap Pair device with pairing code.
5. Enter the shown IP address, pairing port, connect port, and 6-digit code here.
6. Tap Pair + Connect, then use Mute Camera or Unmute Camera.
Tip: if the pairing popup disappears when you switch apps, keep Settings and ShutterMute in split screen. When the popup closes, its pairing port usually stops working too.
Tip: after the first successful pairing, this app keeps its ADB key and can reconnect faster on the same phone later.
Could not detect this phone IP yet. Connect to Wi-Fi, then try again.

What each name means:
- Pairing popup port = the IP:port shown inside the Pair device with pairing code popup
- Wireless debugging port = the IP:port shown on the main Wireless debugging screen
Example: if the popup shows 192.168.0.91:37261 and the main screen shows 192.168.0.91:37267, enter 37261 for Pairing popup port and 37267 for Wireless debugging port.

What each target phone value means:
- Pairing popup port = the target phone popup IP:port
- Wireless debugging port = the target phone main Wireless debugging IP:port
Example: if the target phone popup shows 192.168.0.91:37261 and the target main screen shows 192.168.0.91:37267, enter 37261 for Pairing popup port and 37267 for Wireless debugging port.
Use the IP:port shown inside the Pair device with pairing code popup.
Use the IP:port shown on the main Wireless debugging screen under the device name.
Use the 6-digit Wi-Fi pairing code shown in the popup.
Pairing failed. Recheck the popup code and pairing popup port.
Pairing failed. The pairing popup port is not accepting connections.
Unable to connect to target phone.
Connection failed. Pair first using the popup port and 6-digit code.
Enter the required pairing details and a 6-digit pairing code.
Enter a valid host and connect port.
This phone IP is not ready yet. Connect to Wi-Fi and refresh the self-host address.
Enter the target phone IP from the Wireless debugging screen.
Enter the popup port shown inside Pair device with pairing code.
Enter the main Wireless debugging port shown under the device name.
Enter the 6-digit popup code exactly as shown.
That shortcut is unavailable on this phone. Open the matching settings page manually from Settings.
Could not open the settings shortcut on this phone.
If pairing failed, reopen the pairing popup and double-check both the popup port and the 6-digit code.
The pairing popup port usually means the popup was closed, timed out, or the wrong port was entered. Use the popup IP:port, not the main Wireless debugging port.
If connection failed, recheck the Wireless debugging port from the main screen under the device name.
The connect port only works after pairing succeeds. Pair again with the popup port and the 6-digit code first.
